questions.question_text: A buyer wants to install a septic system on a rural Delaware lot. What must occur before installation can begin?
questions.correct_answer: A DNREC site evaluation of soil conditions
distractors: County zoning board approval only
A federal EPA environmental audit
Written consent from adjacent landowners
questions.explanation: DNREC requires a site evaluation assessing soil and site conditions before any septic system installation in Delaware. This ensures the system will function properly and protect groundwater. The evaluation examines percolation rates, soil depth, and proximity to water sources.

questions.question_text: Which Delaware agency regulates wastewater disposal and septic system permitting?
questions.correct_answer: DNREC
distractors: Delaware Housing Authority
Department of Health and Social Services
Division of Professional Regulation
questions.explanation: The Department of Natural Resources and Environmental Control (DNREC) is the state agency responsible for regulating wastewater systems, septic permits, and environmental compliance in Delaware. All septic installations, repairs, and failures must go through DNREC.

questions.question_text: A homeowner discovers her septic system is failing and leaking into the yard. What is the homeowner's legal obligation?
questions.correct_answer: Have it repaired immediately under a DNREC permit
distractors: Repair it privately without notification
Notify only the nearest neighbor
Wait until the annual inspection cycle
questions.explanation: Under DNREC's on-site wastewater regulations (7 DE Admin. Code 7101), repair or replacement of a malfunctioning septic system must be initiated immediately, and no one may repair a malfunctioning system without first obtaining a Repair or Component Replacement Permit from DNREC — typically arranged through a licensed system contractor. A failing system poses groundwater contamination risks, and fixing it quietly without DNREC permitting is illegal.

questions.question_text: A developer plans to build a large manufacturing plant along the Delaware coastline. Which law most directly restricts this development?
questions.correct_answer: The Coastal Zone Act
distractors: The Clean Air Act
The Delaware Fair Housing Act
The Uniform Land Use Code
questions.explanation: Delaware's Coastal Zone Act limits heavy industrial development in designated coastal areas to protect environmental quality and public health. It was one of the first state laws of its kind in the nation. Developers must obtain permits and meet strict standards before building in the coastal zone.

questions.question_text: A listing agent in Delaware prepares a disclosure package for a home in a FEMA flood zone. What must the seller disclose?
questions.correct_answer: The property's location within a flood zone
distractors: Only the annual flood insurance premium
The name of the nearest emergency shelter
The date of the last major flood event
questions.explanation: Delaware requires sellers to disclose that a property is located in a designated flood zone. This allows buyers to evaluate flood risk, obtain required flood insurance, and make informed purchase decisions. The disclosure is part of the seller's duty under state law.

questions.question_text: A manufactured housing community owner receives a purchase offer from a developer. Who has the right to make the first offer on the community?
questions.correct_answer: The community's residents
distractors: The local municipality government
The state housing authority
Adjacent commercial property owners
questions.explanation: Delaware's Manufactured Home Owners and Community Owners Act gives the residents, through their homeowners' association, a right of first offer when a community owner decides to sell (25 Del. C. §§7030-7036). The owner must send the required notice to the association, and if the owner later would sell to a third party at a lower price, the association gets 30 days to match it. This protects residents from displacement and gives them a chance to purchase the community.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases a manufactured home that sits on a permanent foundation with the land. How is this home classified in Delaware?
questions.correct_answer: Real property
distractors: Personal property titled through DMV
Commercial chattel property
Leasehold improvement only
questions.explanation: In Delaware, a manufactured home permanently affixed to land is classified as real property and conveyed by deed. When not permanently affixed, it remains personal property titled through the DMV. This distinction affects financing, taxation, and transfer procedures.

questions.question_text: A Delaware HOA sends a letter prohibiting a homeowner from installing rooftop solar panels. Can the HOA enforce this restriction?
questions.correct_answer: No, Delaware limits HOA restrictions on solar panels
distractors: Yes, HOAs have full authority over exteriors
Yes, if the restriction predates state law
No, but only for ground-mounted systems
questions.explanation: Delaware law limits the ability of HOAs and deed restrictions to prohibit solar panel installations. This policy supports renewable energy adoption. While HOAs may impose reasonable aesthetic guidelines, they cannot effectively ban solar installations on residential properties.

questions.question_text: A homeowner with a solar lease sells her Delaware property. What happens to the solar lease?
questions.correct_answer: The lease transfers with the property
distractors: The lease is automatically terminated
The seller must buy out the lease first
The buyer must negotiate a new lease
questions.explanation: A solar lease is a contract with the solar company, and in a typical Delaware sale it is transferred to the buyer by assumption: the buyer agrees to take over the existing lease terms, usually subject to the solar company's approval. The alternative is for the seller to buy out or prepay the lease. The lease is not automatically terminated, and the buyer does not sign a brand-new lease. Sellers must disclose the lease, and buyers should review its terms before closing.

questions.question_text: A lender takes a security interest in a Delaware manufactured home that has not been affixed to land. Where is the lien recorded?
questions.correct_answer: Noted on the DMV certificate of title
distractors: In the county recorder of deeds' land records
With the Delaware Manufactured Home Relocation Authority
No recording is possible for manufactured homes
questions.explanation: A manufactured home that is not permanently affixed to land is personal property titled through the Delaware Division of Motor Vehicles, so a lender's security interest is noted on the DMV certificate of title (21 Del. C. Ch. 23) — not in the county land records. Once the home is permanently affixed to owned land and converted to real property, liens attach through the land records like any other mortgage.

questions.question_text: A seller agrees to finance part of a Delaware home purchase. The buyer gives the seller a mortgage for the remaining balance. What type of financing is this?
questions.correct_answer: A purchase money mortgage
distractors: A reverse mortgage arrangement
A home equity line of credit
A wraparound blanket mortgage
questions.explanation: When a buyer gives a mortgage directly to the seller for part of the purchase price, this creates a purchase money mortgage. The seller effectively acts as the lender, and the buyer makes payments according to the agreed terms. This arrangement is common when traditional financing is difficult to obtain.

questions.question_text: A Delaware seller provides financing to a buyer but does not record the mortgage. What risk does the seller face?
questions.correct_answer: A later lender could take priority over the seller
distractors: The buyer can void the entire agreement
The state will impose a recording penalty
The mortgage becomes a personal loan automatically
questions.explanation: Failing to record a purchase money mortgage leaves the seller vulnerable to losing priority. If the buyer later obtains another mortgage from a lender who records first, that lender's lien could take priority. Recording protects the seller's interest by providing public notice of the lien.

questions.question_text: A seller-financed note in Delaware includes a balloon payment due in five years. What must the seller disclose about this payment?
questions.correct_answer: The balloon payment must be clearly disclosed
distractors: Balloon payments are prohibited in Delaware
Disclosure is optional for private sales
Only the interest rate needs disclosure
questions.explanation: Balloon payments in seller-financed deals must be clearly disclosed to the buyer. Where federal Truth in Lending rules apply, the amount and due date of the balloon must appear in the loan disclosures, and under the Dodd-Frank seller-financing rules the ability to include a balloon depends on which exclusion the seller qualifies for. Clear disclosure lets the buyer plan for refinancing or payoff and prevents surprise defaults.

questions.question_text: A buyer defaults on a purchase money mortgage held by the seller in Delaware. What remedy is available to the seller?
questions.correct_answer: The seller may pursue foreclosure proceedings
distractors: The seller can reclaim the property immediately
The seller has no legal recourse available
The seller must accept a short sale only
questions.explanation: When a buyer defaults on a purchase money mortgage, the seller has the right to pursue foreclosure just as any other mortgage holder would. Delaware foreclosure is a judicial process requiring court action. The seller cannot simply reclaim the property through self-help measures.

questions.question_text: A seller finances a home sale in Delaware. Which requirement applies to this transaction?
questions.correct_answer: The seller must comply with lending disclosures
distractors: The seller is exempt from all disclosure rules
Only licensed lenders can hold mortgages
The buyer waives all consumer protections
questions.explanation: Seller-financed transactions in Delaware must comply with applicable lending disclosure requirements. Even though the seller is not a traditional lender, the buyer is entitled to clear terms regarding interest rates, payment schedules, and default provisions to make an informed decision.

questions.question_text: A Delaware property owner notices a separate drainage assessment collected with the county property tax bill. What are these assessments called?
questions.correct_answer: Tax ditch assessments
distractors: Stormwater utility surcharges
Municipal improvement bonds
County flood mitigation fees
questions.explanation: Tax ditch assessments are levied by Delaware's tax ditch organizations — governmental subdivisions created under 7 Del. C. Ch. 41 — to maintain drainage infrastructure. They are collected by the county along with county property taxes but are a distinct assessment that runs with the land, so buyers should be informed the obligation transfers with ownership.

questions.question_text: A buyer's lender requires a boundary survey before closing on a Delaware home. Is a survey legally mandatory for all residential sales in Delaware?
questions.correct_answer: No, but lenders and title companies may require one
distractors: Yes, state law mandates surveys for all sales
Yes, but only for properties over one acre
No, and lenders cannot require one either
questions.explanation: Delaware does not legally mandate surveys for all residential real estate transactions. However, lenders and title insurance companies commonly require them to confirm boundaries, identify encroachments, and ensure clear title. Obtaining a survey is considered best practice even when not required.

questions.question_text: A real estate brokerage places a large advertising sign at a busy Delaware intersection that partially blocks drivers' view of oncoming traffic. Which law addresses this situation?
questions.correct_answer: The Clear Zone law
distractors: The Delaware Billboard Regulation Act
The Highway Beautification Act
The Commercial Signage Ordinance
questions.explanation: Delaware's Clear Zone law (17 Del. C. §§524-527) lets DelDOT maintain clear zones within highway rights-of-way and immediately remove nonofficial signs, poles, and other obstructions that create hazards — including signs that interfere with driver visibility at intersections. Real estate professionals must keep signs out of the right-of-way clear zone to avoid removal, citations, and potential liability.

questions.question_text: A Delaware buyer and seller agree to sign the purchase contract electronically. Under UETA, what is required for the electronic signatures to be valid?
questions.correct_answer: Both parties must consent to electronic signatures
distractors: Only the buyer must consent to the method
A notary must witness the electronic signing
Electronic signatures require court approval first
questions.explanation: Under the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA) adopted by Delaware, electronic signatures are legally valid when both parties consent to conduct the transaction electronically. Neither party can be forced to use electronic signatures, and certain documents such as wills may still require wet signatures.

questions.question_text: A new owner discovers that the previous owner's tax ditch assessment remains unpaid. Who is responsible for this obligation?
questions.correct_answer: The new owner, because assessments run with the land
distractors: The previous owner retains all liability
The drainage district absorbs the cost
Neither party if it was not disclosed
questions.explanation: Tax ditch assessments in Delaware run with the land, meaning they transfer to the new owner regardless of who incurred them. This is why title searches should identify outstanding drainage district assessments before closing. Buyers should budget for these ongoing obligations.
